notes: Under-covered tier: $1000. Pack weighted toward $800 and $1000.

Thin subcategories covered: Physics (Radioactivity, Doppler effect, Absolute zero, Superconductivity, Entropy), Units/Laws/Constants (Kelvin), Biology (Mitochondria, Mitosis, Natural selection), Chemistry (Periodic table, Polonium), Astronomy (Black hole), Scientists (Darwin, Newton).

questions.clue: Nicknamed the 'powerhouse of the cell' in a 1957 Scientific American article, this double-membraned organelle generates adenosine triphosphate through aerobic respiration to supply chemical energy throughout the cell.

citations.title: Mitochondria
citations.snippet: A mitochondrion is an organelle found in the cells of most eukaryotes, such as animals, plants and fungi. Mitochondria have a double membrane structure and use aerobic respiration to generate adenosine triphosphate (ATP), which is used thro

questions.clue: His 1859 work 'On the Origin of Species' introduced the theory of natural selection and is considered one of the most transformative books in the history of science; he was honored by burial in Westminster Abbey.

citations.title: Charles Darwin
citations.snippet: Charles Robert Darwin was an English naturalist, geologist, and biologist, widely known for his contributions to evolutionary biology. His proposition that all species of life have descended from a common ancestor is now generally accepted

questions.clue: Observed by Henri Becquerel in 1896, this property of certain unstable atomic nuclei causes them to spontaneously emit particles or energy as they decay; the three most common emission types are named for the first three letters of the Greek alphabet.

citations.title: Radioactive decay
citations.snippet: Radioactive decay is the process by which an unstable atomic nucleus loses energy by radiation. A material containing unstable nuclei is considered radioactive. Three of the most common types of decay are alpha, beta, and gamma decay. The w

questions.clue: Organized from long-wavelength radio waves through microwaves, visible light, and ultraviolet to high-energy gamma rays, this is the complete ordered range of all types of radiation that travel at the speed of light.

citations.title: Electromagnetic spectrum
citations.snippet: The electromagnetic spectrum is the full range of electromagnetic radiation, organized by frequency or wavelength. The spectrum is divided into separate bands, with different names for the electromagnetic waves within each band. From low to

questions.clue: At exactly −273.15°C or −459.67°F, this theoretical temperature minimum marks the lowest point on the Kelvin scale and represents the lowest energy state a system can reach.

citations.title: Absolute zero
citations.snippet: Absolute zero is the theoretically coldest temperature, a state at which a system's internal energy, and in ideal cases entropy, reach their minimum values. The Kelvin scale is defined so that absolute zero is 0 K, equivalent to −273.15 °C

questions.clue: Predicted by Einstein's general theory of relativity, this astronomical object's gravity is so extreme that nothing can escape once it crosses the boundary known as the event horizon.

citations.title: Black hole
citations.snippet: A black hole is an astronomical body so compact that its gravity prevents anything, including light, from escaping. Albert Einstein's theory of general relativity, which describes gravitation as the curvature of spacetime, predicts that any

questions.clue: The familiar rise and fall in pitch of an ambulance siren as it approaches and then passes a stationary listener is the classic real-world demonstration of this wave phenomenon, named for an Austrian physicist who described it in 1842.

citations.title: Doppler effect
citations.snippet: The Doppler effect is the change in the frequency or, equivalently, the period of a wave in relation to an observer who is moving relative to the source of the wave. It is named after the physicist Christian Doppler, who described the pheno

questions.clue: His 1687 Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica established classical mechanics and is often described as the most influential work in the history of physics; he also independently developed calculus and made foundational contributions to optics.

citations.title: Isaac Newton
citations.snippet: Sir Isaac Newton was an English polymath who was a mathematician, physicist, astronomer, alchemist, theologian, author and inventor. He was a key figure in the Scientific Revolution and the Enlightenment that followed. His book Philosophiæ

questions.clue: Defined so that its zero point corresponds to the theoretically coldest possible temperature and its degrees are the same size as Celsius degrees, this is the base unit for temperature in the International System of Units.

citations.title: Kelvin
citations.snippet: The kelvin is the base unit for temperature in the International System of Units (SI). The Kelvin scale is an absolute temperature scale that starts at the lowest possible temperature, taken to be 0 K. By definition, the units of the Celsiu

questions.clue: This grid-like arrangement of the 118 known chemical elements, organized in order of increasing atomic number, places elements with similar properties in the same vertical column — realizing a law first proposed by Dmitri Mendeleev.

citations.title: Periodic table
citations.snippet: The periodic table, also known as the periodic table of the elements, is an ordered arrangement of the chemical elements into rows ("periods") and columns ("groups"). An icon of chemistry, the periodic table is widely used in physics and ot

questions.clue: During this type of cell division, a eukaryotic cell separates its replicated chromosomes into two new nuclei, producing daughter cells that are genetically identical to the parent.

citations.title: Mitosis
citations.snippet: Mitosis is a part of the cell cycle in eukaryotic cells in which replicated chromosomes are separated into two new nuclei. Cell division by mitosis is an equational division which gives rise to genetically identical cells in which the total

questions.clue: Jointly announced by Charles Darwin and Alfred Russel Wallace in 1858, this evolutionary mechanism describes how individuals with traits better suited to their environment tend to survive and reproduce, gradually shifting a population's inherited characteristics over generations.

citations.title: Natural selection
citations.snippet: Natural selection is the differential survival and reproduction of individuals due to differences in the relative fitness endowed on them by their own particular complement of observable characteristics. It is a key law or mechanism of evol

questions.clue: Named for the homeland of the woman who co-discovered it in 1898 by extracting it from uranium ore on the basis of its unusual emission intensity alone — with no conventional chemical test — this metallic element has atomic number 84.

citations.title: Polonium
citations.snippet: Polonium is a chemical element; it has symbol Po and atomic number 84. A rare and highly radioactive metal with no stable isotopes, polonium is a chalcogen and chemically similar to selenium and tellurium, though its metallic character rese

questions.clue: First observed in mercury by Heike Kamerlingh Onnes in 1911, this physical phenomenon occurs when a material is cooled below a characteristic critical temperature and its electrical resistance drops abruptly to exactly zero.

citations.title: Superconductivity
citations.snippet: Superconductivity is a set of physical properties observed in superconductors: materials where electrical resistance is exactly zero and magnetic fields are expelled from the material. Unlike an ordinary metallic conductor, whose resistance

questions.clue: A thermodynamic state variable measuring the probabilistic distribution of accessible microscopic configurations of a system, this quantity — which can never decrease in an isolated system — underpins the second law of thermodynamics.

citations.title: Entropy
citations.snippet: Entropy is a thermodynamic state variable that quantifies the probabilistic distribution of accessible microstates in a system. The term and the concept are used in diverse fields, from classical thermodynamics, to the microscopic descripti
